section.title: The Wrath of God Revealed
section.sectionRationale: Having announced that the gospel reveals the righteousness of God, Paul now shows why such good news is needed: the wrath of God is being revealed against all human godlessness. He traces the deepest human sin to its root, the suppression of the truth about God that is plainly displayed in creation, and the exchange of the Creator's glory for idols. Three times he says God gave them over, handing rebels to the very desires they chose, until the passage ends in a sober catalog of a debased mind. This is the universal human problem the gospel answers.
section.thesis: The wrath of God is justly revealed against a humanity that knows God from creation yet suppresses the truth and exchanges His glory for idols; and God's righteous judgment is expressed by giving rebels over to their chosen desires, leaving all people without excuse and in desperate need of the righteousness the gospel supplies.
section.frameNote: The indictment unfolds in five movements: the wrath of God revealed against suppressed truth; the great exchange of God's glory for images; God giving them over to impurity; God giving them over to dishonorable passions; and God giving them up to a debased mind, ending in a catalog of sins that all people know deserve death.

units.label: The wrath of God revealed
units.note: God plainly known, so no one is without excuse

verses.text: The {{orge|wrath}} of God is being revealed from heaven against all the godlessness and wickedness of men who suppress the {{aletheia|truth}} by their wickedness.
For what may be known about God is plain to them, because God has made it plain to them.
For since the creation of the world God’s invisible qualities, His eternal power and divine nature, have been clearly seen, being understood from His workmanship, so that men are without excuse.

units.label: The great exchange
units.note: the glory of God traded for images

verses.text: For although they knew God, they neither glorified Him as God nor gave thanks to Him, but they became futile in their thinking and darkened in their foolish hearts.
Although they claimed to be wise, they became fools,
and exchanged the {{doxa|glory}} of the immortal God for images of mortal man and birds and animals and reptiles.
Therefore God {{paradidomi|gave them over}} in the desires of their hearts to impurity for the dishonoring of their bodies with one another.
They exchanged the truth of God for a lie, and worshiped and served the creature rather than the Creator, who is forever worthy of praise! Amen.
For this reason God gave them over to dishonorable passions. Even their women exchanged natural relations for unnatural ones.
Likewise, the men abandoned natural relations with women and burned with lust for one another. Men committed indecent acts with other men and received in themselves the due penalty for their error.

units.label: Given up to a debased mind
units.note: the third handing over and its catalog

verses.text: Furthermore, since they did not see fit to acknowledge God, He gave them up to a {{adokimos|depraved}} mind, to do what ought not to be done.
They have become filled with every kind of wickedness, evil, greed, and depravity. They are full of envy, murder, strife, deceit, and malice. They are gossips,
slanderers, God-haters, insolent, arrogant, and boastful. They invent new forms of evil; they disobey their parents.
They are senseless, faithless, heartless, merciless.
Although they know God’s righteous decree that those who do such things are worthy of death, they not only continue to do these things, but also approve of those who practice them.

orge.gloss: Wrath, settled anger, the just and holy opposition of God to all evil. This is not petulant rage but the righteous response of a good God to sin, His refusal to be indifferent to the ruin of what He made. The wrath revealed here is the dark backdrop against which the righteousness of God in the gospel shines.

aletheia.gloss: Truth, reality as it actually is, especially the truth about God plainly shown in creation. Fallen humanity does not merely lack this truth but actively suppresses it, holding it down like a spring forced under water. To exchange this truth for a lie is the essence of idolatry.

doxa.gloss: Glory, weight, radiant honor; the visible splendor of God's own being. Humanity was made to reflect and enjoy this glory, but in sin traded the glory of the immortal God for images of created things. Every idol is an attempt to replace the incomparable glory of God with something smaller and manageable.

paradidomi.gloss: To hand over, deliver up, give into another's power. Three times God gives sinners over to the desires they have chosen, a judicial abandonment in which He removes His restraint and lets sin run its course. This handing over is itself a present expression of wrath, sin becoming its own punishment.

adokimos.gloss: Failing the test, unfit, disqualified, worthless. Because they refused to see God fit to acknowledge, God gave them over to a mind that is itself unfit, unable to judge rightly. There is a fitting justice in it: a mind that rejected the knowledge of God becomes a mind that no longer works as it should.

basic.context: This passage begins the long argument of Romans by establishing the universal need for the gospel. Before Paul can show how God saves, he must show why salvation is needed. He describes humanity in rebellion, not as ignorant but as knowing God through creation and then suppressing that knowledge, so that the wrath of God is justly revealed against all.
basic.hermeneutics: Read this as the first half of a case that runs through Romans 3, proving that the whole world, Gentile here and Jew in chapter 2, is guilty before God. Notice the repeated words: they exchanged and God gave them over. The structure is a downward spiral, sin leading to judgment leading to more sin. It is meant to silence every mouth and drive us to grace.
basic.originalLanguages: The orge, the wrath, of God is revealed against those who suppress the aletheia, the truth. They traded the doxa, the glory, of God for idols, so God practiced paradidomi, handing them over, three times, until they were left with an adokimos, an unfit and disqualified, mind.
basic.typology: The passage recalls the fall and the golden calf: as Adam and later Israel exchanged the glory of God for a lie and an image, so all humanity repeats the pattern. The idolatry condemned here reverses creation, where humanity was made in God's image to reflect His glory rather than fashion gods in the image of creatures.
basic.christ: Christ is the answer this dark passage requires. He is the true image of God who did not suppress the truth but embodied it, the one who bore the wrath revealed here so that those given over to sin might be given a new heart. The righteousness of God in the gospel is the only rescue from the condemnation this text describes.
basic.god: This passage is God-centered even in describing sin. God is the Creator plainly revealed, the one whose glory is exchanged, the righteous Judge whose wrath is revealed, and the one who in holy justice gives rebels over. His forbearance in the present handing over still leaves the door open for the mercy of the gospel.
basic.crossReferences: Genesis 3 tells the first exchange of God's word for a lie. Psalm 19 and Acts 14:17 affirm creation's witness to God. Ephesians 2:3 calls all people by nature children of wrath. Romans 3:23 sums up the verdict: all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God.
basic.oneStory: The Bible's story of a good creation gone wrong is compressed here. Humanity, made to know and glorify God, suppressed the truth and worshiped the creature, and God justly gave them over. This is the plight the whole of Scripture answers, the reason the gospel of Romans 1:16-17 is such good news, and the darkness against which the coming righteousness of God shines.

context.literary: Verses 18 to 32 open the long argument that human beings are under sin and need the righteousness of God. The passage is tightly structured around two refrains: they exchanged, describing human rebellion, and God gave them over, describing His judicial response. It forms the first movement of an indictment that continues through chapter 2 and reaches its verdict in chapter 3.
context.historical: Paul writes to a church in the heart of the Roman empire, surrounded by temples, idols, and the imperial cult, where the very sins he lists were on public display and often celebrated. His description would have been recognized at once, echoing the way Jewish writers of the time described the Gentile world, yet Paul will soon turn the argument on the self-righteous as well.
context.canonical: The passage draws deeply on the Old Testament's account of idolatry and echoes Genesis, the Psalms, and the prophets. It supplies the theological foundation for the doctrine of universal sinfulness and the need for grace. Its sober realism about wrath and judgment guards the gospel from sentimentality and sets up the great therefore of justification in chapters 3 to 5.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To demonstrate that all people, beginning with the idolatrous Gentile world, stand justly under the wrath of God, so that no one can claim innocence and everyone is shown to need the righteousness God offers in the gospel. Paul writes to expose the depth of the human problem before displaying the greatness of the remedy.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: Paul is describing the actual condition of fallen humanity and God's just response, not prescribing behavior. The abiding truth is diagnostic: this is what sin is and where it leads, and it is prescriptive only in driving every reader to seek the mercy of God rather than to condemn others self-righteously, as chapter 2 immediately warns.

debates.question: How should we understand God gave them over?

views.label: Judicial abandonment
views.case: The historic reading sees God actively withdrawing His restraining grace and handing sinners to the consequences of their own chosen desires; this is a real act of judgment, not mere permission, yet God is not the author of their sin.

views.label: Bare permission only
views.case: Some soften it to God merely allowing sin to take its course; this preserves God's holiness but underplays the deliberate, judicial force of the verb Paul repeats three times.

debates.bearing: The stronger reading takes wrath seriously: God's present judgment can consist in giving people up to the sin they insist on, so that sin becomes its own punishment, which makes the rescue of the gospel all the more urgent.

debates.question: Is the wrath in verse 18 a present reality or only future?

views.label: A present revealing with a future consummation
views.case: Paul says the wrath is being revealed now, chiefly in the handing over he describes, while a final day of wrath still awaits, as Romans 2:5 makes clear; present and future are held together.

views.label: Purely future judgment
views.case: Some restrict wrath to the last day; but Paul's present tense and the threefold giving over show wrath already at work in history, even as its full disclosure is still to come.

debates.bearing: Seeing wrath as a present reality, not only a distant threat, presses the urgency of the gospel now and explains the moral disintegration Paul catalogs as judgment already unfolding.

study.originalLanguages: The passage turns on a few weighty words. The orge of God, His settled holy anger, is being revealed against those who suppress the aletheia, the truth about God plain in creation. At the root is idolatry: they traded the doxa, the glory, of the immortal God for images. In response God enacts paradidomi three times, handing them over to impurity, to dishonorable passions, and finally to an adokimos mind, a mind that has failed the test and can no longer judge what is fitting. The vocabulary shows sin and judgment interlocked: the rejection of God issues in a self that no longer works as God made it to.

typology.fulfillment: As Adam and Eve exchanged the truth of God for the serpent's lie, so humanity here exchanges the truth of God for a lie and the Creator for the creature, Genesis 3.

typology.type: The golden calf and Israel's idolatry
typology.fulfillment: Israel exchanged the glory of God for the image of an ox that eats grass; Paul universalizes the charge, showing all humanity trades God's glory for images, Psalm 106:20.
Humanity, made in God's image to reflect His glory, inverts creation by making gods in the image of creatures; only Christ, the true image of God, restores what was lost, Colossians 1:15.

study.christ: This passage names the disease that only Christ can cure. Where humanity suppressed the truth, Christ is the truth; where they exchanged God's glory, Christ is the radiance of that glory; where they were given over to sin and its penalty, Christ was given up to death and bore the wrath revealed from heaven. The catalog of a debased mind is the very ruin from which the crucified and risen Lord redeems. Read rightly, the darkness of Romans 1 makes the mercy of the gospel blaze.
study.god: The passage reveals God as Creator, plainly known through what He has made, and as righteous Judge, whose wrath against evil is real and just. His glory is the treasure humanity foolishly trades away, and His holiness is the standard by which sin is measured. Yet even His giving over is restrained and purposeful, not the end of the story but the exposure of need, so that the same God may reveal the righteousness that saves. He remains forever worthy of praise, as verse 25 confesses.

crossReferences.note: The heavens declare the glory of God, so that creation's witness leaves humanity without excuse.
Israel exchanged their glorious God for the image of an ox, the very exchange Paul universalizes.
All people were by nature children of wrath, dead in sin, describing the condition Romans 1 exposes.
All have sinned and fall short of the glory of God, the verdict this indictment builds toward.

study.oneStory: The whole Bible tells of a good world made to glorify its Maker, wrecked when humanity suppressed the truth and worshiped created things. Romans 1 gathers that story into a single indictment: the glory of God exchanged for idols, and God justly giving rebels over to their desires. This is the ruin the gospel comes to reverse. The righteousness revealed in Christ answers the wrath revealed here, so that people made to reflect God's glory can be restored to it at last.

quiz.q: Why does Paul say people are without excuse before God?

options.text: Because God's eternal power and divine nature are clearly seen in creation, yet they suppress that truth.
options.note: Verses 19 and 20 say God's qualities are plain in creation, leaving people without excuse.

options.text: Because they never had any way of knowing God at all.
options.note: The opposite is true: what may be known of God is plain to them, which is why they are without excuse.

options.text: Because they broke the law of Moses given to Israel.
options.note: Law-breaking does condemn, but Paul's argument here concerns the witness of creation to all people; he takes up the law later.

options.text: Because they failed to attend proper worship.
options.note: The issue is suppressing the truth about God known through creation, not neglecting religious rituals.

quiz.q: What is the root exchange Paul describes at the heart of human sin?

options.text: They exchanged the glory of the immortal God for images of created things.
options.note: Verse 23 names idolatry, trading God's glory for images, as the fundamental exchange.

options.text: They exchanged wealth for poverty by unwise living.
options.note: The exchange Paul names is theological, God's glory for idols, not a financial one.

options.text: They exchanged the truth of God for a lie in their thinking.
options.note: This is genuinely part of it, verse 25, and flows from the idolatry, though the root exchange named in verse 23 is trading God's glory for images.

options.text: They exchanged old traditions for new customs.
options.note: The real exchange is the glory of God for images, an act of idolatry, not mere cultural change.

quiz.q: What does the repeated phrase God gave them over mean?

options.text: God, in judgment, handed rebels over to the desires they chose, so that sin became its own punishment.
options.note: The threefold handing over is a present, judicial expression of God's wrath.

options.text: God withdrew His restraint and let their chosen sin run its course.
options.note: Very close: this captures the judicial abandonment, though Paul's verb stresses an active handing over, not mere withdrawal.

options.text: God simply stood by with no involvement at all.
options.note: The handing over is an active, judicial act, not divine absence or indifference.

options.text: God forced people to sin against their will.
options.note: God gave them over to desires they already had; He is not the author of their sin.

quiz.q: How does this passage function within Paul's larger argument in Romans?

options.text: It exposes the universal human problem of sin and wrath that the gospel's righteousness answers.
options.note: Paul is proving the need for the gospel by showing all are under wrath.

options.text: It shows that Gentile idolaters stand under God's judgment, beginning the case that all are guilty.
options.note: Right as far as it goes: this is the first stage, but Paul extends the indictment to the respectable and to Israel in the chapters that follow.

options.text: It offers a program of moral self-improvement.
options.note: The passage diagnoses the problem; the remedy is the righteousness of God by faith, not self-improvement.

options.text: It denies that God cares about human behavior.
options.note: The revealing of wrath shows God cares deeply about evil and its ruin.

quiz.q: What is true of those who practice the sins Paul catalogs in verses 29 to 32?

options.text: They know God's righteous decree that such things deserve death, yet continue and even approve of others who do them.
options.note: Verse 32 stresses their knowledge of the verdict and their approval of evil.

options.text: They know such things are wrong but do them anyway in weakness.
options.note: They do know the decree, but Paul says more: they not only continue but openly approve of others who practice such things.

options.text: They act in complete ignorance of any moral standard.
options.note: Paul says they know God's righteous decree, so it is not sheer ignorance.

options.text: They are excused because everyone behaves this way.
options.note: Universality does not excuse; Paul says they are worthy of death and without excuse.

questions: Paul says the truth about God is plain in creation, yet people suppress it. Where do you see this suppression at work today, and where might you be tempted to it yourself?
The root of sin here is exchanging the glory of God for lesser things. What functional idols compete for the worship your heart owes to God alone?
God's wrath is revealed partly by giving people over to the desires they insist on. How does this sober picture of sin becoming its own punishment change the way you view your own struggles with sin?
This passage insists that no one is without excuse. How does facing the depth of the human problem make the righteousness of God in the gospel more precious to you?
Verse 32 warns not only against doing evil but approving those who do it. Where are you tempted to applaud or excuse what God calls wrong, and how should the gospel reshape that?
Christ bore the wrath this passage describes so that idolaters might be restored to God's glory. How does that move you to worship the Creator who is forever worthy of praise?

translationNotes.note: Who suppress the truth by their wickedness renders a verb meaning to hold down or restrain; the picture is of actively pressing the truth about God out of sight, not passively lacking it.

God gave them over translates a strong verb meaning to hand over or deliver up. Repeated in verses 26 and 28, it describes a judicial act of God in which He withdraws restraint and delivers sinners to their chosen desires.

A depraved mind renders a word meaning failing the test, unfit, or disqualified. There is a deliberate play: because they did not see fit to acknowledge God, God gave them up to an unfit mind, a mind that can no longer judge rightly.
